era_context: The reign of Theodosius I in the Eastern Roman Empire; the Western Empire is ruled by the young Valentinian II under the guardianship of the military strongman Arbogastes. Theodosius is consolidating Christian dominance and suppressing pagan practices. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty is in decline, with regional warlords gaining autonomy. This is a period of religious transformation in the Roman Empire and political fragmentation in China.

events.title: Theodosius I issues decrees suppressing pagan religious practices
events.description: In 391 CE, Theodosius I issued a series of decrees (or reinforced earlier ones) that suppressed pagan religious practices throughout the Roman Empire. These edicts banned pagan sacrifices, closed temples, and prohibited pagan worship in public spaces. The decrees represented the culmination of decades of Christian imperial policy and marked a decisive shift toward Christian monopoly on religious practice. However, enforcement was uneven, and pagan practices continued in rural areas and among the aristocracy. The decrees were not universally popular, even among Christians, and sparked resistance from pagan traditionalists.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The decrees are preserved in the Theodosian Code. The exact dating of individual edicts is sometimes uncertain (they may span 391–395), but the general policy is confirmed for 391.

events.title: Destruction of the Serapeum of Alexandria
events.description: In 391 CE (or possibly 392), a Christian mob, possibly incited by Patriarch Theophilus of Alexandria, destroyed the Serapeum, one of the most important pagan temples in the Roman Empire. The Serapeum housed the famous Library of Alexandria (or a portion of it) and was a center of pagan learning and worship. The destruction was a symbolic and practical blow to paganism and represented the growing power of Christian mobs and bishops to act against pagan institutions. The exact date and circumstances are debated by modern scholars, but the destruction occurred in the early 390s CE.
key_figures: Theophilus of Alexandria (Patriarch, possibly inciting the destruction)
Pagan traditionalists and philosophers (defending the temple)
Theodosius I (emperor, whose policies enabled the destruction)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Eunapius of Sardis, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The destruction of the Serapeum is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date (391 or 392) is debated. The circumstances and the role of Theophilus are also disputed by modern scholars.
cross_references: Related to 391-001 (Theodosius' suppression of pagan practices)
Part of broader pattern of Christian violence against pagan institutions

events.title: Valentinian II rules Western Roman Empire under Arbogastes' guardianship
events.description: In 391 CE, Valentinian II, the young Western Roman Emperor (age 20), continued to rule under the effective guardianship of the military strongman Arbogastes. Arbogastes controlled the armies, the treasury, and the administration, while Valentinian II was increasingly marginalized. This arrangement was unstable and would collapse in 392 with Valentinian II's death and Eugenius' usurpation. The period exemplified the declining power of the imperial office and the rising power of military strongmen in the late Roman Empire.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Valentinian II's nominal rule and Arbogastes' effective control are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Eastern Jin dynasty continues fragmentation; regional warlords consolidate power
events.description: In 391 CE, the Eastern Jin dynasty in southern China continued to experience internal fragmentation and loss of central authority. Regional warlords and military commanders (jiedushi) consolidated power in their territories, effectively becoming independent rulers. The imperial house was increasingly ceremonial, with real power held by competing generals. This period of fragmentation would accelerate the dynasty's decline and lead to its collapse in 420 CE.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Eastern Jin fragmentation is confirmed by Chinese sources, but specific events in 391 are approximate.

events.title: Northern Wei dynasty consolidates administrative and military control in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei dynasty, founded in 386 CE, continued to consolidate its administrative and military control over northern China in 391 CE. Under Emperor Hongwu (Daowu), the Northern Wei conducted reforms to strengthen the state apparatus and conducted military campaigns to expand territory. The dynasty was becoming the dominant power in the north, setting the stage for eventual reunification of China under Northern Wei rule.
events.certainty_note: The general consolidation of Northern Wei power is confirmed, but specific events in 391 are approximate.

events.title: Hypatia of Alexandria begins her career as mathematician and philosopher
events.description: In 391 CE (or possibly slightly earlier or later), Hypatia of Alexandria, one of the most famous mathematicians and philosophers of antiquity, was active in Alexandria. Hypatia taught mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy at the Neoplatonic school in Alexandria and made contributions to geometry and the study of conic sections. She was a pagan philosopher in an increasingly Christian city and would eventually be killed by a Christian mob in 415 CE. Her career exemplified the declining status of pagan learning in the late Roman Empire.
key_figures: Students and patrons of the Neoplatonic school
events.certainty_note: The exact date of Hypatia's birth and the beginning of her career are uncertain. Sources suggest she was born around 350–370 CE and was active by the 390s CE, but the precise dating is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the destruction of the Serapeum as a triumphant Christian victory is complicated by modern scholarship: (1) The exact date is uncertain; sources differ between 391 and 392 CE. (2) The role of Theophilus of Alexandria is debated; he may have incited the destruction, but sources are unclear. (3) The destruction may have been a riot rather than an organized religious campaign. (4) The Library of Alexandria may have already been destroyed or dispersed before 391; the Serapeum's library was not the famous Library of Alexandria. (5) Hypatia's career dating is uncertain; she may have begun teaching slightly earlier or later than 391. (6) The characterization of Theodosius' pagan suppression as 'complete' is oversimplified; pagan practices persisted for centuries.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 391 CE is moderate in richness. Christian sources (Socrates, Theodoret, Orosius) provide narrative but are written 50–200 years after the events and are shaped by Christian triumphalism. Pagan sources are sparse; Eunapius and Damascius provide some pagan perspective but are also late compilations. The Theodosian Code provides primary evidence for imperial legislation. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence for 391 CE is minimal. The record is heavily shaped by Christian historiography and imperial propaganda.

graph_edges.note: Theodosius' decrees against pagan practices enabled the destruction of the Serapeum
Theodosius' suppression of pagan practices led to the suppression of the Olympic Games
The destruction of the Serapeum exemplified the Christian suppression of pagan learning that culminated in Hypatia's death
Valentinian II's weak position under Arbogastes led to his death and Eugenius' usurpation
Eastern Jin fragmentation and Liu Yu's rise led to Liu Song dynasty
